title: Says Mister Doojabs
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, eight months ago one clear cold day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I took a ramble up Broadway,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with my hands behind my back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I strolled along on the streetcar trackן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (I walked on the track, for walking there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gives one, I think, a distinguished air.)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Well, all of a sudden I felt a jar
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I said, ן¿½Iן¿½ll bet thatן¿½s a trolley car,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, sure enough, when I looked to see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw it had run right over me!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And my limbs and things were so scattered about
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That for a moment I felt put out.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, the motorman was a nice young chap!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he came right up and tipped his cap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And said, ן¿½Beg pardon,ן¿½ and was so kind
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That his gentle manner soothed my mind:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Especially as he took such pains
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To gather up my spilt remains.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, he found my arms and found my head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then, in a contrite voice, he said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Say, mister, I guess Iן¿½ll have to beg Your pardon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I canן¿½t find your left leg,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he would have wept, but I said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½No! no! It doesnן¿½t matter, just let it go.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, I went on home and on the way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I considered what my wife would say:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I knew she would have some sharp reply
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If I let her know I was one leg shy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So I thought, on the whole, ן¿½twould be just as well
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For my peace of mind if I didnן¿½t tell.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, that was the first thing in my life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I kept a secret from my wife.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And for eight long months I was in distress
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To think that I didnן¿½t dare confess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½d probably still feel just that way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If it hadnן¿½t come ן¿½round to Christmas Day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, in good old customs I still believe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So I hung up my stocking Christmas Eve;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (A brand-new left one Iן¿½d never worn.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when I looked in it Christmas morn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was my leg, as large as life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a ticket on it, ן¿½From your wife.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, my wife had had it stored away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In cotton, since last Easter Day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When she ran across it, quite by chance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the left hip-pocket of my pants;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the only reproachful thing she said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was, ן¿½Look out or some day youן¿½ll lose your head.ן¿½
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Ellis Parker Butler delivers a powerful performance in "Says Mister Doojabs"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
